title: Ask Me Anything: A simple strategy for prompting language models

structured.summary: The paper proposes ASK ME ANYTHING (AMA), a novel prompting strategy that converts tasks into open-ended QA prompts generated recursively by the LLM itself and aggregates multiple noisy prompt outputs using weak supervision without labeled data. AMA demonstrates significant performance improvements across 20 benchmarks and 14 models, enabling smaller open-source LLMs to rival much larger few-shot GPT-3 baselines.

strengths.evidence: The paper empirically shows open-ended QA prompts outperform restrictive prompts across tasks and models, aligning better with LLM pretraining distributions (Section 4.1).
Introduces functional prompt-chains (question() and answer()) to generate diverse QA prompts from inputs without manual engineering (Section 4.2).

strengths.point: Weak supervision aggregation without labeled data
strengths.evidence: Applies weak supervision to model prompt accuracies and dependencies, outperforming majority vote by up to 8.7 accuracy points and enabling reliable aggregation (Section 4.3, Section 5.2).
Evaluated on 20 benchmarks and 14 LLMs (125M-175B parameters), showing AMA enables GPT-J-6B to match or exceed GPT3-175B few-shot performance on most tasks (Section 5).
Code, prompts, and detailed diagnostics are released to facilitate reproducibility and analysis (Section 5.4).

weaknesses.point: Limited comparison to related ensemble prompting methods
weaknesses.evidence: Only brief comparison to Self-Consistency prompting; lacks benchmarking against other prompt ensembling or voting-based aggregation approaches (Section 5.3).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient methodological details on weak supervision model
weaknesses.evidence: Omission of WS model architecture, training procedure, hyperparameters, and convergence criteria hinders reproducibility (Section 4.3).

weaknesses.point: Evaluation baselines use different shot counts
weaknesses.evidence: Few-shot GPT3 baselines use 32-70 examples, while AMA uses zero-shot recursive prompts, making comparisons not strictly apples-to-apples (Section 5.1).

weaknesses.point: Lack of analysis on prompt diversity impact
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation quantifies whether prompt diversity or quantity drives performance gains (Section 4.2).

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation on knowledge-intensive and multilingual tasks
weaknesses.evidence: AMA shows smaller gains on closed-book factual tasks and no experiments on multilingual or cross-lingual settings (Section 5.2).

weaknesses.point: Missing statistical significance and variance reporting
weaknesses.evidence: Results report average accuracy improvements without confidence intervals or significance tests (Section 5).

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility hindered by missing prompt generation details
weaknesses.evidence: Lack of pseudocode or detailed prompt templates for recursive question() and answer() functions (Section 4.2).

weaknesses.point: Unclear availability of weak supervision code and data
weaknesses.evidence: Uncertainty whether WS aggregation code, model checkpoints, and unlabeled data are publicly released (Section 4.3).
The term 'prompt-chains' is not clearly defined or distinguished from standard prompt ensembles or chain-of-thought prompting (Section 4.2).

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on handling long contexts and instruction errors
weaknesses.evidence: Acknowledges challenges but lacks quantitative analysis or mitigation strategies (Section 6).

questions.question: How does AMA compare to other ensemble or voting-based prompt aggregation methods beyond Self-Consistency prompting?
questions.motivation: Understanding relative advantages and novelty requires benchmarking against a broader set of related methods.

questions.question: To what extent does prompt diversity versus sheer number of prompts contribute to AMA’s performance gains?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this can guide efficient prompt generation strategies and reduce computational overhead.

questions.question: Can AMA’s recursive prompt generation and weak supervision aggregation generalize effectively to multilingual or non-English tasks?
questions.motivation: Extending AMA beyond English is critical for broader applicability in diverse real-world settings.

questions.question: What are the detailed architectures, training procedures, and hyperparameters of the weak supervision model used for aggregation?
questions.motivation: Providing these details is essential for reproducibility and adoption by the research community.

questions.question: How does AMA perform when applied to generation tasks such as summarization or dialogue, which may require different prompt formats?
questions.motivation: Evaluating generalizability beyond classification and QA tasks would demonstrate broader utility.

critique_points.point_001: Limited comparison to related ensemble prompting methods. Only brief comparison to Self-Consistency prompting; lacks benchmarking against other prompt ensembling or voting-based aggregation approaches (Section 5.3)
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient methodological details on weak supervision model. Omission of WS model architecture, training procedure, hyperparameters, and convergence criteria hinders reproducibility (Section 4.3)
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation baselines use different shot counts. Few-shot GPT3 baselines use 32-70 examples, while AMA uses zero-shot recursive prompts, making comparisons not strictly apples-to-apples (Section 5.1)
critique_points.point_004: Lack of analysis on prompt diversity impact. No ablation quantifies whether prompt diversity or quantity drives performance gains (Section 4.2)
critique_points.point_005: Limited evaluation on knowledge-intensive and multilingual tasks. AMA shows smaller gains on closed-book factual tasks and no experiments on multilingual or cross-lingual settings (Section 5.2)
critique_points.point_006: Missing statistical significance and variance reporting. Results report average accuracy improvements without confidence intervals or significance tests (Section 5)
critique_points.point_007: Reproducibility hindered by missing prompt generation details. Lack of pseudocode or detailed prompt templates for recursive question() and answer() functions (Section 4.2)
critique_points.point_008: Unclear availability of weak supervision code and data. Uncertainty whether WS aggregation code, model checkpoints, and unlabeled data are publicly released (Section 4.3)
critique_points.point_009: Terminology ambiguity. The term 'prompt-chains' is not clearly defined or distinguished from standard prompt ensembles or chain-of-thought prompting (Section 4.2)
critique_points.point_010: Limited discussion on handling long contexts and instruction errors. Acknowledges challenges but lacks quantitative analysis or mitigation strategies (Section 6)
